nodes.intent: Configuration has one source of truth: the repo. Every change flows through a branch, a PR, and a pipeline. Nothing is set manually.
Every resource type is denied until the platform has security controls and observability wired. Approval is the platform's commitment, not a task left to the application team.
Self-service, self-contained landing zones. The application team owns it end-to-end — what they build and how they run it is their call.
Platform-managed infrastructure carries no flat fees. Every service the platform provisions scales to zero when idle — fixed monthly costs are eliminated by design.

nodes.mechanism: Each application has a dedicated billing scope; every subscription it provisions bills to it exclusively.
nodes.why: Tags, filters, and manual splits are allocation logic — they drift. A dedicated billing scope makes cost accuracy structural, not maintained.

links.note: Application Profile registration creates the invoice section.
A landing zone costs nothing until the application team deploys — every line on their invoice is their own spend, not platform overhead.

nodes.mechanism: Only Azure Resource Manager (ARM) reachable services are eligible.
nodes.why: Without ARM reachability, the control plane has no reach — deny-by-default, declarative lifecycle, and diagnostic coverage all fail at once.

links.note: ARM-native is the prerequisite for deny-by-default to hold.
Self-service exemptions are viable because every resource is within Azure Policy's reach — a non-ARM service sits outside Policy's scope entirely.

nodes.mechanism: Every resource is denied by default. It appears on the approved list only after the platform has whitelisted.
nodes.why: Without whitelisting, deny policies and diagnostic settings are assumed to be wired for every resource type — the approved list is what turns that assumption into a guarantee.

links.note: Must use an Allowed-prefixed assignment for resource-type compliance.
Every approved type must have a diagnostic settings definition.
New resource type triggers review of whether function-scoped roles need new actions.
Hard deny becomes a bottleneck without a self-service exemption path — teams push back and the deny gets softened.
New resource type with no built-in Deny policy requires a custom definition before security coverage is complete.
ARM-reachability is the prerequisite for allowlisting — types outside ARM are ineligible.
Whitelisting triggers a Defender check — paid-SKU recommendations or remediation that requires cost go on the exemption list.

nodes.mechanism: When no built-in policy provides the required Deny or DeployIfNotExists effect, a custom definition is authored to close the gap.
nodes.why: Audit looks like coverage but the violation still lands — a custom definition with the required effect is the difference between a guardrail that enforces and one that observes.

links.note: The naming convention constrains custom definitions to Deny or DeployIfNotExists/Modify effects — the effect determines the assignment prefix.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy assignment names are [deny|config|allowed]-[Requirement].
nodes.why: Without a predictable naming pattern, teams cannot identify the correct assignment to exempt without a portal lookup — self-service breaks down and the platform team becomes the bottleneck.

links.note: Predictable names are what make exemptions self-service — teams can identify the correct assignment by name, no portal access required.

nodes.mechanism: Policy exemptions source assignment IDs from the generated reference file — never a hardcoded ARM ID.
nodes.why: Assignment ARM IDs change when a policy is redeployed; a hardcoded ID silently breaks every exemption that references it.

links.note: Stable, predictable names are the keys the reference file is indexed by — they don't change when a policy is redeployed.

nodes.mechanism: Every platform production deployment passes through a test environment first. This is enforced by branch protection rules and workflow structure.
nodes.why: Without an enforced gate, a single push can change production infrastructure with no prior validation.

links.note: Promotion gate targets oases-test for validation.

nodes.mechanism: Configuration has one committed source of truth — a single JSON file in the repo. Values are propagated to GitHub variables and Bicep parameters at deployment time. Nothing is set manually in the UI or injected out of band.
nodes.why: Without one committed source, environments drift. Onboarding a new engineer means tracing values through the GitHub UI instead of reading a file.

links.note: New repos receive a subset of platform variables at creation.
Per-environment client IDs sourced from this file.
GitHub App ID sourced from this file.
githubVariables.json must be committed with initial platform cinfig values before BigBang first runs.

nodes.mechanism: Deployment Stacks use deleteAll lifecycle. Any Azure resource no longer declared in the Bicep template is deleted on the next deployment. The template is source of truth — what's in the code is what exists in Azure.
nodes.why: deleteAll is what makes the template authoritative — without it, decommissioned subscriptions carry ghost resources, reuse is unsafe, and every teardown becomes a manual investigation.

links.note: deleteAll in test verifies code produces the expected Azure state before promotion to prod.
Single resource group is the stack's scope deleteAll on it is a complete teardown.
A subscription after deleteAll is guaranteed empty makes subscription reuse safe.
ARM-native resources are the only ones stack-managed azure-native-services-only is the prerequisite for deleteAll lifecycle enforcement.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy enforces hard denials across security disciplines on every resource type that supports each: public network access, local authentication, minimum TLS version, and cross-tenant replication.
nodes.why: Without enforcement, misconfiguration is invisible — nothing distinguishes a wrongly configured resource from an intentional one.

links.note: All disciplines deploy as deny- prefixed assignments — the prefix is what makes the enforcement category identifiable without a portal lookup.
Hard denial across disciplines is only sustainable if teams can unblock themselves — without it, operational pressure erodes each deny one at a time.
Where built-in policies provide only Audit effect for a discipline, a custom definition is required to close the enforcement gap.
Managed identity provides the Entra ID path that replaces local authentication — the deny on local auth is only viable because this identity path exists.

nodes.mechanism: Gating and execution are separate workflow layers. Trigger workflows own conditions, branch rules, and environment gates. Template workflows own deployment logic. The two are never merged — a trigger calls a template, never the reverse.
nodes.why: Mixing gating with execution forces the same deploy logic to be duplicated per environment. A bug fix in deployment logic becomes a multi-file change.

links.note: Reusable workflow separation is how end-to-end deployment achieves consistent execution logic without duplication across environments.
Gating lives in the trigger layer only.

nodes.mechanism: Each platform capability owns its Bicep modules and GitHub workflows end-to-end, so changes are fully isolated.
nodes.why: Without isolation, a change to one capability risks triggering or breaking another's deployment — the blast radius of every PR becomes unpredictable.

links.note: deleteAll on each capability's stack ensures no orphaned resources after a declaration is removed.
Each capability has its own isolated promotion path.
Capability configuration is sourced from the repo, not injected ad hoc.
Each capability deploys to the test hierarchy before production.

nodes.mechanism: The platform has a manual bootstrapping boundary. BigBang requires platform identity, GitHub App, subscription bank, and management group hierarchy to exist before its first run — none of which automation can create.
nodes.why: BigBang cannot bootstrap its own prerequisites — it needs identity to authenticate, a hierarchy to deploy into, and a subscription bank to draw from. These have no automated path.

links.note: App registration created, OIDC federation configured, and role assigned at the management group.
Microsoft Graph roles granted to the app registration via admin consent.
GitHub App created, installed in the org, and org-level secrets and variables seeded.
subscription-bank management group needs to be created.
gazelle-test and gazelle-prod management groups need to be created.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zone creation automatically allocates each VNet a unique CIDR from the platform-managed address space.
nodes.why: Fixing an address overlap means re-wiring network.

links.note: Address space is allocated without regional segmentation.

nodes.mechanism: Custom roles are scoped to job function, not resource type. A role describes what a person does (platform engineer), not what resources they touch. When new capabilities require new actions, existing roles are extended — new roles are not created.
nodes.why: Resource-specific roles multiply with every new type until auditing them becomes impractical. Function-scoped roles grow in actions, not in count.

links.note: New resource type triggers review of whether roles need new actions.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a dedicated Action Group that wires budget and activity log — to contact details sourced from the application profile. The team that owns the landing zone receives every notification.
nodes.why: Hardcoded contacts go stale as teams change. Alerts fire to former members, incidents go unanswered, and nobody knows until production is affected.

links.note: Contact details sourced from the Application Profile details.
Budget alerts are routed to the team that owns the spend.

nodes.mechanism: Registering an Application Profile is how an application joins the platform and gains the ability to provision its own landing zones.
nodes.why: Registration is the anchor — without it there is no billing scope for cost ownership, no contact details for alerting, and no repo for self-service. Everything downstream assumes it exists.

links.note: At landing zone creation, the subscription from the bank moves to this profile's invoice section.
The IDs created at registration (Entra group, invoice section) live as GitHub Variables on the application repo.
The GitHub App creates the application repo from the landingzone-template and seeds it with platform variables.
Engineer contact email from the profile is the action group's notification target.
Owner and engineer emails from the profile are the tag baseline values injected at deployment time.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a dedicated automation runtime running inside its own subscription. Job definitions and images are centrally managed by the platform, but execution happens locally.
nodes.why: Centralized automation requires a shared management subscription with baseline cost and team dependency. The platform has neither — automation runs where the landing zone lives.

links.note: Tag remediation runs as a landing-zone-native job.
Container Apps free monthly minutes cover jobs that complete in seconds.
Container Apps environment is region-bound; a second region requires a separate managed environment inside each landing zone.

nodes.mechanism: Every whitelisted resource type has diagnostic settings deployed automatically via DeployIfNotExists policy. Telemetry flows to the landing zone's Log Analytics workspace with no team action required.
nodes.why: Opt-in telemetry produces gaps that surface only during an incident.

links.note: Must use a Config-prefixed assignment for diagnostic settings.
Diagnostic settings target only ARM-native resource types  nativity is a prerequisite for telemetry coverage.

nodes.mechanism: GitHub self hosted agents run inside the landing zone network, giving workflows private access to Azure services.
nodes.why: Public network access is denied on every service. A public GitHub runner has no network path to the data plane.

links.note: Public runners have no data-plane path when public network access is denied — self-hosted runners inside the zone are the only viable executor.
Runners attach to the landing zone VNet and reach PaaS services through service endpoints.
Each landing zone registers its own runner, keeping execution isolated within its own workflow.
GitHub Actions service and subnet must be in one of supported regions.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a single managed identity that serves all its capabilities — GitHub workflows, automation jobs, and Entra ID queries.
nodes.why: Without a single identity, each capability needs its own RBAC and OIDC config. The permission surface multiplies with every new capability and auditing becomes impractical.

links.note: Managed identity is the runtime identity for all automation jobs.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zone provisioning draws from a subscription bank. Decommissioned subscriptions return to the bank after deleteAll guarantees they are empty. New subscriptions are created only when the bank is empty.
nodes.why: MCA quota counts active and cancelled subscriptions equally. Always creating new subscriptions exhausts the quota ceiling — reuse is the only sustainable path.

links.note: Reused subscription must move to the application's invoice section before provisioning completes.
subscription-bank management group must exist before the first landing zone can be provisioned.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a dedicated Log Analytics workspace with retention within the free-tier boundary.
nodes.why: A shared workspace merges ingestion costs across landing zones. Attributing a cost to application requires additional tooling.

links.note: Diagnostic settings are platform-defined — the platform controls what ingests into this workspace and that cost lands on the application team's bill.
Defines what telemetry flows into this workspace.
Log Analytics workspace is regional.

nodes.mechanism: Platform-managed landing zone resources live in a single resource group, protected by an Azure Deployment Stack.
nodes.why: A shared resource group blends platform and application resources. No clean boundary exists for teardown, RBAC, or incident management when both share the same scope.

links.note: Managed identity is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
Resource group is the Deployment Stack scope — deleteAll tears down every platform-managed resource in one operation.
Log Analytics workspace lives inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
VNet is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by platform Deployment Stack
Container Apps environment and jobs are provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment S tack

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy enforces required tags on every resource. Tag values are sourced from the application profile and remediated nightly — they are never manually authored.
nodes.why: Manually applied tags drift within weeks. When an incident fires, the email points to someone who left the team and the alert goes unanswered.

links.note: Nightly tag remediation runs as a Container App Job.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zones are stamped from a single template, but operated through its own parameter file and workflow.
nodes.why: Without it, teams cannot act on their landing zone without risking another's redeployment.

links.note: Landing zone initial values come from the application profile.
App team owns the deployment workflow — execution delegates to the platform's reusable template.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone gets a dedicated VNet with a CIDR allocated by IPAM. PaaS services connect via service endpoints — private endpoints are not used because they add fixed hourly cost per endpoint.
nodes.why: Without a dedicated VNet, network routing and configuration become shared decisions requiring cross-team coordination.

links.note: Service endpoints provide the secure network path that satisfies the deny on public network access.
IPAM allocates the VNet's address spcae — prevents overlap across landing zones.
Gives GitHub workflows private network access to PaaS services.
VNet is provisioned in AZURE_DEFAULT_LOCATION; a second region requires a new VNet and a separate regional IPAM allocation.

nodes.mechanism: The platform uses GitHub Container Registry as the single shared image source for all automation jobs across all landing zones — an explicit exemption from the azure-native-services-only constraint.
nodes.why: Azure Container Registry carries a flat monthly cost per instance. One registry per landing zone multiplies that cost linearly with zone count, breaking the zero-cost-on-idle guarantee.

links.note: Exemption exists to preserve this guarantee.
Automation runtime this exemption covers.
Azure Container Registry has a fixed cost per landing zone.
Outgoing traffic to GitHub Container Registry does not violate the public network access deny.

nodes.mechanism: A single Entra ID app registration is the platform identity. It provisions landing zones, assigns Graph permissions to their managed identities.
nodes.why: A managed identity requires an Azure resource as its host. The platform has no subscription of its own — an app registration exists in Entra ID independent of any Azure infrastructure.

links.note: Platform identity provisions each landing zone's managed identity — federated credentials are scoped per repo and environment.

nodes.mechanism: A single GitHub App serves as the platform's GitHub-side identity. All platform workflows and landing zone deployments authenticate to GitHub through it — each workflow mints its own short-lived token from the same App.
nodes.why: Without a dedicated GitHub identity, cross-repo and org-level operations have no viable auth path.

links.note: OIDC federation is the Azure auth path; this App covers GitHub-side operations.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone identity receives Directory.Read.All at creation time. This lets automation jobs query Entra ID for users, groups, and principals.
nodes.why: Without Directory.Read.All, landing zone automation cannot detect deleted principals or resolve group membership. Role cleanup jobs fail silently and stale assignments accumulate.

links.note: Receives Directory.Read.All at LZ creation time.
Performs the appRoleAssignedTo during LZ provisioning.
cleanup-roles is the primary consumer queries Entra ID to detect deleted principals.
Graph permissions is granted via admin consent as a manual prerequisite before BigBang first runs.

nodes.mechanism: Platform changes deploy to a prod-identical test management group hierarchy before reaching production. It is where failures are safe to land.
nodes.why: Without a parallel hierarchy,  platform changes hit production directly.

links.note: Test hierarchy is the target the promotion gate validates against.
Template workflow changes are validated in the test environment.
Registration workflow is validated here before any platform change reaches production.
New whitelisted resources are manually tested here before reaching production.
Test has its own dedicated app registration, sourced from a GitHub variable.
Same Graph permissions as production.
Test and production share the same GitHub App.

nodes.mechanism: The platform provides a set of Defender for Cloud exemptions. These cover recommendations where remediation requires a paid SKU or infrastructure with flat cost.
nodes.why: Unexempted recommendations with no free remediation path inflate the compliance backlog with items nobody can act on. The score becomes noise.

links.note: Defender exemptions follow the same long-lived exemption mechanism — PR is the review and audit record.
Defender for cloud paid SKU lands on the subscription billing.
Whitelisting a resource type triggers a Defender recommendation check new recommendations requiring paid SKUs are candidates for the exemption list.

nodes.mechanism: Policy exemptions are either long-lived or temporary — each mode has a distinct audit trail.
nodes.why: The hard deny is only sustainable if teams can unblock themselves — without it, deny-by-default erodes under operational pressure.

links.note: Long-lived exemptions reference assignment IDs from the generated file — without it, authoring an exemption requires portal access.

nodes.mechanism: One region is what keeps availability model, network topology, and service parity a single shared answer across all capabilities.
nodes.why: Without it, region overhead lands on every Azure operation and service parity becomes an engineer decision, rather than a platform guarantee.

links.note: Region is defined once in githubVariables.json; all deployments fetch the value from there.
GitHub runners VNet integration is region-gated; the platform region must support it.
